obsidian_markdown:
# RPL Attack Detection and Prevention in the Internet of Things Networks Using a GRU Based Deep Learning

## Summary
This paper addresses the increasing threat of cyberattacks on Internet of Things (IoT) networks, specifically targeting the Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RPL). The study proposes a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) based deep learning model to detect and prevent Hello Flooding (HF) attacks on RPL, enhancing network security. The model's performance is compared against Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Logistic Regression methods, demonstrating superior effectiveness in source efficiency and security with a lower error rate in attack detection.

## Contributions
- Proposed a GRU-based deep learning model for detecting and preventing Hello Flooding attacks in RPL protocols.
- Compared the performance of the proposed model with SVM and Logistic Regression methods, demonstrating improved efficiency and security.
- Analyzed different power states and total energy consumptions of nodes to ensure robustness and reliability of the model in IoT networks.

## Method
The study employs a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network model for deep learning to predict and prevent Hello Flooding attacks on RPL protocol. The performance of this model is evaluated against Support Vector Machine and Logistic Regression methods, considering various power states and energy consumption levels of the nodes in IoT networks.

## Results
The proposed GRU-based model showed superior performance in terms of source efficiency and IoT security. It achieved a significantly lower error rate in detecting HF attacks compared to existing methods, confirming its effectiveness in protecting RPL protocol from attacks.

## Limitations
The paper does not specify limitations or future work explicitly. However, potential limitations could include the need for further validation in diverse real-world IoT environments and the computational cost associated with deep learning models.

## Keywords
Internet of Things, RPL attacks, GRU-based deep learning, Hello Flooding, IoT security, Source efficiency, Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression
